analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 7 finding(s) from 8 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: The AES key is readable by any bus master; no access control is present at the peripheral level. Only the interconnect may gate transactions, but the access_ctrl values themselves might be writable (see OBL-003/006), so this is a direct exposure.
findings.vulnerability_category: read / AES 192-bit key (key_big, driven by key_in) / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: Combinational read mux exposing key_big slices at address offsets 16-21.
key_big is directly wired to key_in with no masking.
Read path is a combinational always @(*) block without any privilege or lock condition.

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Any bus master (CPU, debug AXI master via JTAG/DMI) Operation: read Object: AES 192-bit key (key_big, driven by key_in) Expected guard: Privilege level check, lock bit, or debug authentication state at the AES wrapper or APB level Observed behavior: The AES wrapper unconditionally exposes all 192 bits of the key on the read data bus through a combinational case statement with no privilege checks or lock bits. Path: AXI bus → axi2apb_64_32 → apb_to_reg → reg_bus_aes → aes_wrapper.external_bus_io.rdata (address offsets 16-21) Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Full extraction of AES key by any bus master that can access the AES address range, compromising all encrypted data.

findings.summary: Intermediate AES state is accessible to any bus master; no guard at peripheral level. This exposes internal cryptographic state.
findings.vulnerability_category: read / AES intermediate state (inter_state[127:0]) / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: Combinational read mux exposing inter_state slices at address offsets 22-25.
Read path is a combinational always @(*) block without any privilege or lock condition.

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Any bus master (CPU, debug AXI master) Operation: read Object: AES intermediate state (inter_state[127:0]) Expected guard: Privilege check or lock to prevent observation of ongoing computation Observed behavior: The AES wrapper unconditionally exposes the full 128-bit intermediate state through a combinational read mux with no privilege checks or locks. Path: AXI bus → axi2apb_64_32 → apb_to_reg → reg_bus_aes → aes_wrapper.external_bus_io.rdata (address offsets 22-25) Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Leakage of intermediate AES state, enabling side-channel attacks or key recovery.

findings.summary: Both bootrom and rom2 lack hardware write protection. The write paths are active and reachable over AXI, enabling corruption of boot code and security-critical keys.
findings.vulnerability_category: write / bootrom memory content (mem) and rom2 secure_reg (key/access_ctrl storage) / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: Always_ff block with write path assigning to mem array when we_i is high.
mem is declared as logic, not const, allowing writes.
Always_ff block with write path to secure_reg when we_i is high. mem is const but secure_reg is writable.
CRitical registers (jtag_key, AES key, access_ctrl) are stored in writable secure_reg.

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Any bus master with write access to ROM/ROM2 address ranges Operation: write Object: bootrom memory content (mem) and rom2 secure_reg (key/access_ctrl storage) Expected guard: Hardware-based write protection (we_i tied to 0 or address-space write-lock) Observed behavior: bootrom.sv includes an active write path (mem[addr] <= wdata_i) when we_i is asserted. rom2.sv allows writing to secure_reg after reset. No write protection or lock bit is present. Path: For bootrom: AXI → axi2mem → rom_req/rom_we → bootrom.we_i → mem write (line 263). For rom2: AXI → axi2mem → rom2_req/rom2_we → rom2.we_i → secure_reg write (line 42). Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Boot code corruption in bootrom; modification of cryptographic keys and access control registers in rom2, enabling full privilege escalation.

findings.summary: The debug AXI master path exists and can reach all peripherals, but whether it requires authentication cannot be confirmed without debug module internals.
findings.vulnerability_category: any memory-mapped read/write / All peripherals (AES, ROM, ROM2, PLIC, UART, SPI, Ethernet, DRAM) / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: Debug AXI master connected to slave[1] of the interconnect.

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Debug AXI master (via JTAG/DMI → dm_top → slave[1]) Operation: any memory-mapped read/write Object: All peripherals (AES, ROM, ROM2, PLIC, UART, SPI, Ethernet, DRAM) Expected guard: Debug authentication / lock state (e.g., secure debug module with sticky unlock) Observed behavior: The debug module's AXI master is connected to the interconnect's slave[1] port. A jtag_key signal is provided to dmi_jtag, but the internal authentication logic of dm_top and dmi_jtag is not inspectable. Path: JTAG → SimJTAG → dmi_jtag → dm_top → dm_axi_m_req → slave[1] → axi_node → peripheral masters. Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If authentication is absent or bypassable, the debug path grants complete unrestricted access to all memory-mapped resources.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Internal RTL of dm_top and dmi_jtag not available to verify if jtag_key is used for authentication before enabling AXI transactions.

findings.summary: The jtag_key register is writable via the ROM2 interface without any lock mechanism, violating the expected guard.
findings.vulnerability_category: write / jtag_key register (key_reg_out[2]) / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: Always_ff block allowing writes to secure_reg entries.
jtag_key output is connected to key_reg_out[2].

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Any bus master with write access to ROM2 address range Operation: write Object: jtag_key register (key_reg_out[2]) Expected guard: Lock bit or write-once semantics after first write, or write restricted to secure boot phase Observed behavior: The jtag_key is stored in writable secure_reg of rom2 (line 42). No lock bit or write-once mechanism is present. Any bus master that can write to ROM2 can modify the jtag_key. Path: AXI → axi2mem → rom2_req/rom2_we → rom2.we_i → secure_reg[addr] <= wdata_i. The jtag_key is output from key_reg_out[2] (line 213-215). Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Attacker can overwrite jtag_key to a known value, potentially enabling unauthorized debug access and complete system compromise.

findings.summary: Privilege-level based access control may exist but cannot be confirmed without the mapping module's RTL.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write / Configuration registers or memory of AES, ROM, or other protected slaves / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: connectivity_mapping module instantiated with priv_lvl_i and access_ctrl_i.
priv_lvl wire from processor connected to interconnect.

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: User-mode bus master (CPU) or any master not asserting machine privilege Operation: read/write Object: Configuration registers or memory of AES, ROM, or other protected slaves Expected guard: Address-range-based privilege check: access to certain addresses must require machine mode privilege Observed behavior: The interconnect contains a connectivity_mapping module that takes priv_lvl_i and access_ctrl_i and produces a connectivity map used by axi_node. The internal mapping logic is not inspected, so it is unknown whether privilege levels are correctly enforced. Path: CPU → AXI master → slave[0] → axi_node_intf_wrap → connectivity_mapping → axi_node → peripheral master. Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If incorrectly implemented, user-mode code could access machine-mode-only peripherals.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Internal implementation of connectivity_mapping not available; cannot verify that it correctly gates transactions based on priv_lvl and access_ctrl.

findings.summary: Insufficient evidence to confirm that all AES state is cleared on reset.
findings.vulnerability_category: reset / AES internal state and key registers (inter_state, ct, key_big) / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: Reset block clears start, p_c, state, but does not mention inter_state or ct.

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Reset controller / system reset Operation: reset Object: AES internal state and key registers (inter_state, ct, key_big) Expected guard: All security-critical state must be cleared to known values (e.g., 0) on reset Observed behavior: In aes_wrapper.sv, the reset logic clears start, p_c, and state registers. However, inter_state and ct are driven by the aes_192_sed instance, and their reset behavior is not visible. key_big is a combinational assignment, so it does not retain state. Path: rst_ni → aes_wrapper always_ff block (resets some registers) and aes_192_sed instance (unknown reset). Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: Potentially leaking cryptographic state across resets if the AES core does not clear its outputs.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The internal always_ff blocks of aes_192_sed for inter_state and ct are not available; cannot confirm if they are reset.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
